# AQA GCSE English Literature — Unseen Poetry — 10 Worked Past-Paper Pairs (2017–2025)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section C (Unseen Poetry). Q27.1 single-poem analysis (24 marks, ~30 minutes, ~600 words). Q27.2 method comparison to a second printed unseen poem (8 marks, ~12-15 minutes, ~250 words). AOs assessed: AO1 (interpretation, evidence) + AO2 (writer's methods). AO3 (context) and AO4 (SPaG) are NOT assessed in Section C. AQA prints BOTH poems on the paper; the comparison is METHOD, not theme.

## How this Q-bank works

Unlike the named-anthology Q-banks (Power and Conflict, Love and Relationships, Worlds and Lives), Unseen Poetry has no anthology to predict against — AQA prints any two poems from the global poetry corpus each year. What CAN be rehearsed is the procedure. This Q-bank rebuilds the section as ten WORKED PAST-PAPER PAIRS that AQA has actually set (2017 specimen through 2025). Each Q reproduces the real poems AQA printed in that year, then walks through a Grade-9 worked plan for Q27.1 + Q27.2 against those poems. The procedure is fixed; the poem is variable. Whichever pair AQA prints in 2026, the FLAME-scan, TTECEA+C body shape, and method-comparison phrasing pattern carry across.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) are chronological — 2017 specimen first, 2025 last. Rehearsal priority is not chronological; rehearse the pairs whose stem theme matches your weakest method-cluster.

## Past-paper pair index

| Q | Year | Q27.1 anchor (poem A) | Q27.2 comparison (poem B) | Stem theme |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | 2017 Specimen | I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage) | Against Road Building (Sophie Hannah) | Regret / cost of action |

| 2 | June 2018 | On Aging (Maya Angelou) | Jessie Emily Schofield (Judy Williams) | Aging and its effects |

| 3 | June 2019 | The Richest Poor Man in the Valley (Lindsay Macrae) | Nobody (Michael Laskey) | How to live your life |

| 4 | November 2020 | I Am Offering this Poem (Jimmy Santiago Baca) | The sun has burst the sky (Jenny Joseph) | Feelings about love |

| 5 | November 2021 | A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M. (Amy Lowell) | November night, Edinburgh (Norman MacCaig) | A city at night |

| 6 | June 2022 | Shoulders (Naomi Shihab Nye) | Choices (Tess Gallagher) | Care and responsibility |

| 7 | June 2023 | Scaffolding (Seamus Heaney) | Yours (Daniel Hoffman) | The strength of a relationship |

| 8 | June 2024 | The Washing Never Gets Done (Jaan Kaplinski) | A Day in Autumn (R S Thomas) | Daily life and the natural world |

| 9 | June 2025 | Table (Robert Hull) | Like a Beacon (Grace Nichols) | A meaningful object / connection to home |

| 10 | 2017 Specimen — alternative pair | Poem for My Sister (Liz Lochhead) | I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage) | Sibling protection / regret across years |

Pair confidence: Q1–Q9 are confirmed against AQA-published papers or mark schemes. Q10 reuses the 2017 specimen alternate-question pairing teaching resources record from the AQA sample set — included so the student has a tenth worked pair against a different stem.

## FLAME method-first reading — the five-minute exam scan

Students lose marks by rushing to content. The mark scheme rewards close analysis of method. Sophicly's house acronym is FLAME: Form, Language (diction + figurative), Architecture (structure + voltic turn), Music (sound + rhythm), Effect-on-reader. Scan FLAME during the second reading of the poem. Whichever of the five elements is loading the heaviest weight in the poem in front of you becomes the spine of the essay. A method-first reading produces topic sentences that argue concept; a content-first reading produces topic sentences that summarise plot. Topic sentences that summarise plot cap at Band 4.

## Pedagogical depth — two non-negotiables

1. Every Unseen poem rewards reading AGAINST its surface meaning — find the GENERATIVE TENSION.

The Grade-9 reading of any Unseen poem identifies the standard reading (the surface theme the stem names) AND the counter-reading the same evidence supports. For a love poem, find the line that COMPLICATES love. For a poem about how to live, find the line where the speaker's confidence is shadowed by limit. For a poem about a city, find the line where the city becomes alienating. Every poem worth setting at GCSE level contains the line that argues against its surface. The dominant theme is what most students will write about; the GENERATIVE TENSION is what carries the essay past the Band 5 line. Method-first analytical work is the discipline that locates the generative tension — usually inside a single image, a single tonal pivot, a single grammatical inversion, or the volta. The Grade-9 reading does not catalogue evidence FOR the surface theme; it identifies the moment the surface theme is COMPLICATED, then traces the methodological mechanism by which the complication is achieved. Every BP3 (closing-of-poem paragraph), every Q27.1 conclusion, every Q27.2 method-differential should land on the generative tension the poem stages. House move: "The poem presents [surface theme], yet [specific line / image / structural pivot] complicates the surface: [the counter-reading]. The complication is the poem's deepest move."

2. Q27.2's 8 marks reward METHOD comparison, not CONTENT comparison.

This is the most-repeated examiner remark across AQA examiner reports for Section C. Students lose marks by writing "Both poems present love" — that is content comparison, and it is not what Q27.2 asks. Students gain marks by writing "Poem A presents love through accumulating natural-image personification, while Poem B presents love through a sequence of accumulating practical objects. The differential reveals that Poem A stages love as cosmic event and Poem B stages love as continuing material gift." That is method comparison, and it is what Q27.2 asks. Sophicly's house phrasing pattern: "Poem A presents [theme] through [method A], while Poem B presents [theme] through [method B]. The difference reveals [larger argument]." Drill the pattern.

Q27.2 paragraph architecture: Sophicly's house rule. One TTECEA paragraph per four marks. Q27.2 carries eight marks, so the answer is two TTECEA paragraphs at four marks each — not the integrated multi-hinge treatment the thirty-mark anthology Section B question rewards. Paragraph 1 is a full TTECEA on Poem A's method for the stem theme, anchored to a quoted line. Paragraph 2 is a full TTECEA on Poem B's method, opened by a contrast hinge of the form "Whereas Poem A uses [method A], Poem B uses [method B]", and closing on the comparison insight — what the methodological differential reveals about the two poets' larger arguments. Each paragraph runs ~125 words. The whole response runs ~250 words. The sequential shape is faster to plan in the exam and easier for the examiner to follow.

## TTECEA+C paragraph shape — Sophicly's Grade-9 BP scaffold

Each Q27.1 body paragraph runs seven sentences. The structure is the same regardless of theme:

• Topic sentence (concept only) — concept anchored to a specific image or moment in the poem; zero techniques named. Max 30 words. The reader should be able to read sentence 1 and understand the paragraph's conceptual claim about the poem.

• Technique — name the method (form / structure / language / sound), without quoting yet. One method per paragraph; resist multi-prong loading.

• Evidence — quote the anchor line. Ideally a single phrase, not a multi-line block. One anchor quote per paragraph.

• Close analysis — zoom into one specific word, sound, or image from the quote. Word-level not catalogue-level.

• Effects on reader — what the technique installs in the reader (Form-Focus-Emotion chain). Use precise emotion words; avoid generic "sadness" or "interest". Cite the somatic / cognitive / behavioural effect.

• Author's purpose — tentative, hedged ("may have written", "can be read as", "appears designed to"). Root the purpose in textual evidence, not biography.

• +C (alternative interpretation or counter-reading) — the generative tension. The same evidence supports a second reading that complicates the surface; this sentence stages that complication and is the paragraph's deepest move.

Two interchangeable BP architectures. Each Q deploys one of two three-paragraph spines, chosen to fit the poem's heaviest-loading element. Both architectures use the TTECEA+C internal shape; what differs is the axis along which the three BPs are organised.

• BEGINNING / MIDDLE / END (BME). BP1 anchors in the poem's opening, BP2 in the middle or volta, BP3 in the closing image. Use BME when the poem's argument unfolds chronologically — when the closing line genuinely changes what the opening meant. Q2, Q4, Q6, Q8 and Q10 in this bank use BME.

• FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE (FSL). BP1 zooms on form (the whole-poem architecture — sonnet, couplet, stanza-shape, free verse), BP2 on structure (movements, voltic turn, closing pivot), BP3 on language (imagery, diction, sound). Use FSL when the form itself does load-bearing argumentative work and the language-cluster carries the deepest move. Q1, Q3, Q5, Q7 and Q9 in this bank use FSL.

Whichever architecture is chosen, BP3 carries the synthetic load — it is the paragraph that most often lands on the generative tension.

## How to substitute when AQA prints a different poem

The procedure is fixed; the poem is variable. The student walks into the exam holding:

• A methodological vocabulary (FLAME)

• A paragraph shape (TTECEA+C, seven sentences, three paragraphs)

• A method-comparison phrasing pattern (Sophicly's house formula)

• An emotion-word vocabulary (pity, dread, complicity, awe, tenderness, vertigo, indignation, exposure)

Whichever pair AQA prints in 2026, scan FLAME, identify three anchors, spine each BP through the heaviest-loading method element using either the BEGINNING / MIDDLE / END or the FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE architecture, and write. Q27.2: write two sequential TTECEA paragraphs — paragraph 1 on Poem A's method, paragraph 2 on Poem B's method opened by a comparison hinge, each anchored to a quoted line.

## Memorise these once

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple pairs):

• FLAME — Sophicly's house acronym for the first-five-minutes method scan: Form, Language, Architecture, Music, Effect-on-reader.

• Controlling concept — the single claim the poem makes about its subject (not the subject itself). One sentence; if it cannot be stated in one sentence, it is not sharp enough.

• Volta — the structural turn; the moment the poem's argument pivots.

• Voltic structure — any poem that organises itself around a turn; the relationship between before-and-after is usually the controlling concept made structural.

• Cyclical structure — opening and closing on identical or near-identical material; usually argues the situation is unresolved or the lesson must be relearned.

• TTECEA+C — Sophicly's body-paragraph scaffold: Topic sentence, Technique, Evidence, Close analysis, Effect, Author's purpose, +Counter-reading.

• Method-first reading — the discipline of scanning for method during the second reading of the poem, before committing to content.

• Pararhyme — half-rhyme in which consonants match but vowels differ; produces refused resolution.

• Compound neologism — invented multi-word compound; enacts the failure of inherited vocabulary.

• Anaphora — insistent repetition at the start of successive lines or clauses; produces incantation.

• Apostrophe — direct address to an absent person or abstraction.

Universal AO2 phrasing pattern:

• A Band 4 essay says: "the poet uses alliteration."

• A Band 5 essay says: "the poet uses alliteration to replicate the percussion of gunfire."

• A Band 6 essay says: "the poet uses alliteration to replicate the percussion of gunfire, forcing the reader to hear the battlefield rather than read about it — collapsing aesthetic distance and conscripting the reader into the very experience the poem is documenting."

Sophicly content rules — academic register throughout; never use "shows" as analytical verb (use illustrates, portrays, demonstrates, emphasises, highlights, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, enacts, signals, mirrors, dramatises, registers, captures, betrays); concrete prose connectors only (through, from, leading to, which produces); no arrows; topic sentences max 30 words concept-first; sentence cap ~40 words.

Q1 — 2017 Specimen — Regret across years — *I Am Very Bothered* (Armitage) + *Against Road Building* (Hannah)

In 'I Am Very Bothered', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about a past action? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage)

I am very bothered when I think

of the bad things I have done in my life.

Not least that time in the chemistry lab

when I held a pair of scissors by the blades

and played the handles

in the naked lilac flame of the Bunsen burner;

then called your name, and handed them over.

O the unrivalled stench of branded skin

as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in,

then couldn't shake off the two burning rings. Marked,

the doctor said, for eternity.

Don't believe me, please, if I say

that was just my butterfingered way, at thirteen,

of asking you if you would marry me.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a confession of adolescent cruelty. The generative tension is the closing tercet: the speaker offers a marriage proposal as the misread motive for the burning. The poem stages regret AND simultaneously refuses the conventional shape of regret — it converts cruelty into a strained metaphor for desire. The Grade-9 reading does not read the closing as exculpation; it reads it as the speaker's continuing failure to accept what he did. The form (broken sonnet — fourteen lines split 7/4/3) carries the moral verdict: the love-sonnet form is invoked and disfigured.

• Hook: "Armitage's closing reframe — that the burning was a thirteen-year-old's marriage proposal — is the most disturbing line of the poem, because it asks the reader to accept courtship as the alibi for cruelty."

• Building sentence: "The poem is a confessional lyric mounted on a disfigured love-sonnet, fusing imperative-address direct speech, sensory-loaded narrative tercet, and a closing parenthetical that converts the violence into proposal."

• Thesis: "Across a fourteen-line sonnet broken into 7/4/3, a voltic closing tercet whose proposal-frame fails the moment it is articulated, and burning-ring imagery that smuggles the wedding-band into the cruelty three stanzas early, Armitage presents the speaker's regret as a confession that still refuses the verdict it should pronounce."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form is the love-sonnet disfigured, and the disfigurement itself carries the moral verdict before any line of content is read.

• Technique: Sonnet form invoked and broken — fourteen lines split 7/4/3 rather than the inherited octave-sestet, with the closing tercet refusing the formal couplet a love-sonnet expects.

• Evidence: "I am very bothered when I think / of the bad things I have done in my life. / Not least that time in the chemistry lab".

• Close analysis: The seven-line opening stanza overruns the octave's eight-line settlement by one line and stops short by one — neither the inherited shape nor a clean refusal of it; the broken stanza-count signals a form attempting fidelity and failing.

• Effects: The reader registers the form's wrongness somatically — the count is wrong, the closing couplet that should land does not — and the wrongness primes the reader for the moral wrongness the content will deliver.

• Author's purpose: Armitage may be deploying the love-sonnet form precisely because the closing turn will reveal the cruelty was retrospectively framed as courtship; the disfigured form is the formal evidence of the disfigured motive.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the broken sonnet refuses the consolation an unbroken sonnet would grant — the speaker is choosing the form whose breakage matches the harm done, registering accountability through the formal refusal of the love-form he might once have claimed.

• Topic: The poem's structural turn occurs at the final tercet, where the prior eleven lines of confession are retroactively reframed as inarticulate marriage proposal — and the reframe is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure landing on a parenthetical disclosure, paired with the imperative "Don't believe me, please" that pre-emptively destabilises the closing claim the speaker is about to make.

• Evidence: "Don't believe me, please, if I say / that was just my butterfingered way, at thirteen, / of asking you if you would marry me."

• Close analysis: The volta occurs grammatically at "if I say" — the conditional clause that introduces the proposal-frame as something the speaker recognises will not be believed; "butterfingered way" domesticates the cruelty by infantilising the agent, the compound adjective deflecting accountability into clumsiness.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as juror to a defence the speaker himself is asking the juror to reject; the voltic structure produces revulsion rather than the tenderness the proposal-frame would otherwise invite, because the framing arrives discredited.

• Author's purpose: Armitage appears to deploy the voltic closing to expose how perpetrators reshape past harm through retrospective sentiment — the love-frame is staged as the speaker's defence mechanism, not the truth of the action.

• +C: A defensible counter-reading is that the speaker's pre-emptive disclaimer is sincere — he names the inadequacy of the proposal-frame because he knows it cannot redeem the act, and the voltic structure registers the impossibility of confession ever quite catching up with what was done.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is the imagery of branding and rings in the central tercet, which plants the wedding-band symbolism three stanzas before the closing reveals the proposal-frame.

• Technique: Olfactory and tactile sensory imagery loaded into the central stanza, with the noun "rings" doing semantic double-duty — burn-marks and wedding-bands simultaneously.

• Evidence: "O the unrivalled stench of branded skin / as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in, / then couldn't shake off the two burning rings."

• Close analysis: The participle "branded" recruits the vocabulary of livestock-marking and slavery; the noun "rings" foreshadows the wedding-bands the closing tercet will retroactively invoke; the verb "slipped" uses the diction of trying-on jewellery, fusing the cruelty and the proposal at the level of word-choice.

• Effects: The reader is forced into the chemistry lab through smell — olfactory imagery is harder to refuse than visual imagery, because the reader cannot un-smell the imagined scene — and the bodily complicity primes the recognition that the rings of injury are also the rings of marriage.

• Author's purpose: Armitage may be loading the language with proposal-vocabulary at the moment of injury to argue that the speaker's later reframing was not a retrospective fabrication but a continuous self-deception running through the action itself.

• +C: The generative tension lives at the level of language: "two burning rings" is the line where cruelty and courtship are already inseparable, and the poem's deepest move is the demonstration that the disfigured marriage-vocabulary was present in the original injury — the conversion was not added later but never absent.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Armitage deploys the disfigured sonnet, the sensory-loaded middle stanza, and the parenthetical closing proposal to present regret as a confession that has not yet earned the verdict it deserves."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is its refusal of the redemption-arc that confessional poetry typically grants — the speaker remains, at the close, the agent of the metaphor he cannot un-author.

In both 'I Am Very Bothered' and 'Against Road Building', the poets present feelings about an action that has consequences. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these feelings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Against Road Building (Sophie Hannah)

(Note: this comparison poem is named in the AQA Specimen Sample Set 1 teaching resources but is not in the public domain. Approach the methodological hinges below using the generic shape — if the actual paper prints a different second poem, the FLAME-scan + hinge-pair procedure carries across.)

• Topic sentence: Armitage presents the speaker's regret through second-person apostrophe to an absent victim, which stages the cruelty as a private intimacy the reader overhears rather than a public confession.

• Technique: Direct address to "you" sustained across the narrative tercet, fusing victim-address with the reader's involuntary witness-position.

• Evidence: "as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in, / then couldn't shake off the two burning rings."

• Close analysis: The pronoun "you" withholds the victim's name across the entire poem; the participle "slipped" converts the burning into something the victim is staged as having walked into, the address performing the speaker's continuing failure to take grammatical accountability.

• Effects: The reader is conscripted into a private intimacy the victim cannot now refuse — discomfort produced by the apostrophe positioning the reader as the unauthorised audience.

• Author's purpose: Armitage may be using apostrophe to argue that retrospective regret cannot escape the grammar of the original harm.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Armitage works through private apostrophe to a named victim, Hannah works through public address to an abstract civic harm — the road-building project — registering regret as protest against an action not yet completed.

• Technique: Public direct address (reader-as-citizen) deployed in disciplined rhyming form, the form's order performing the moral position.

• Evidence: (per AQA Specimen Sample Set 1: Hannah's poem uses a structured rhyming voice to protest planned roadworks; the printed paper supplies the anchor line.)

• Close analysis: The disciplined rhyme converts the speaker's anger into deliberated rhetoric rather than expostulation; the public address positions the reader as fellow citizen with a stake in the outcome.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into argument rather than overhearing private cruelty — solidarity produced where Armitage produces discomfort.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Hannah may be deploying public address to make resistance possible while harm is still preventable; the differential reveals that Armitage stages consequence as personal mark the speaker cannot un-author, while Hannah stages consequence as public harm the speaker still has time to prevent.

Q2 — June 2018 — Aging and its effects — *On Aging* (Maya Angelou) + *Jessie Emily Schofield* (Judy Williams)

In 'On Aging', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about getting older? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — On Aging (Maya Angelou)

When you see me sitting quietly,

Like a sack left on the shelf,

Don't think I need your chattering.

I'm listening to myself.

Hold! Stop! Don't pity me!

Hold! Stop your sympathy!

Understanding if you got it,

Otherwise I'll do without it!

When my bones are stiff and aching,

And my feet won't climb the stair,

I will only ask one favor:

Don't bring me no rocking chair.

When you see me walking, stumbling,

Don't study and get it wrong.

'Cause tired don't mean lazy

And every goodbye ain't gone.

I'm the same person I was back then,

A little less hair, a little less chin,

A lot less lungs and much less wind.

But ain't I lucky I can still breathe in.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a defiant rejection of pity. The generative tension is the closing quatrain's tonal pivot from imperatives ("Hold! Stop!") to gratitude ("ain't I lucky I can still breathe in") — the speaker who refused sympathy has nothing left to refuse except thankfulness for the breath itself. The poem's controlling concept is not anti-pity but pro-dignity; Angelou converts the older speaker from object-of-care to subject-of-her-own-life, and the form (cascading imperatives giving way to lyric flat statement) enacts the conversion.

• Hook: "Angelou's closing pivot — from imperative refusal of pity to the rhetorical question 'ain't I lucky I can still breathe in' — converts an older speaker's defiance into a humility she has earned the right to feel."

• Building sentence: "The poem is a vernacular lyric of aged self-assertion, fusing cascading imperatives, conditional clauses that catalogue physical limit, and a tonal pivot from protest to gratitude across four loosely-rhymed stanzas."

• Thesis: "Across cascading imperatives that refuse the witness's right to interpret stillness, vernacular diction that refuses inherited register, and a closing quatrain that converts cumulative subtraction into thankfulness for the breath itself, Angelou presents the speaker's feelings about aging as a defiant reclamation of self that earns the right to its final humility."

• Topic: The opening stanza claims the speaker's right to her interior life by refusing the witness's right to interpret her stillness.

• Technique: Cascading imperatives loaded back-to-back with exclamation marks, paired with the simile that names the misreading.

• Evidence: "When you see me sitting quietly, / Like a sack left on the shelf, / Don't think I need your chattering. / I'm listening to myself. / Hold! Stop! Don't pity me!"

• Close analysis: The simile "a sack left on the shelf" names the misreading the witness makes — stillness mistaken for emptiness; the imperatives "Hold! Stop!" stage the speaker's act of refusing the misreading the moment it begins.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as the chattering witness — the "you" implicates whoever is reading — and the imperatives produce shock rather than recognition; the reader must reposition before the second stanza begins.

• Author's purpose: Angelou may be staging the imperatives as a rhetorical recovery of authority — the speaker who would be overlooked claims the grammatical position of command, refusing the passive position the witness assigned.

• +C: The cascading imperatives can also be read as the speaker's exhaustion at having to defend the obvious — the volume signalling the cumulative weight of being misread across years.

• Topic: The middle stanzas catalogue physical limit without surrender, claiming dignity through the refusal of one specific symbol of aged passivity.

• Technique: Conditional clauses ("When my bones are stiff and aching") plus vernacular diction ("don't bring me no rocking chair") refusing standardised English.

• Evidence: "When my bones are stiff and aching, / And my feet won't climb the stair, / I will only ask one favor: / Don't bring me no rocking chair."

• Close analysis: The rocking chair is a cultural symbol of aged retirement; the double negative "don't bring me no" refuses both the chair and the register the chair belongs to — a register that would silence the speaker into a different grammar than her own.

• Effects: The reader registers the vernacular as the speaker's chosen authority — her dignity lives inside her language, not above it; the double negative produces respect rather than correction.

• Author's purpose: Angelou appears to fuse African-American vernacular with the lyric form to assert that the speaker's authority does not require translation into standardised English; the form's hybridity is the argument.

• +C: The rocking chair as the only refused object suggests that the speaker accepts every other physical limit aging brings — her dignity is selective, not absolute; she names exactly what she will not become.

• Topic: The closing quatrain converts the defiance of the opening into gratitude for the most basic continuance, and the conversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Tonal pivot from imperative to declarative paired with the rhetorical question that closes the poem.

• Evidence: "I'm the same person I was back then, / A little less hair, a little less chin, / A lot less lungs and much less wind. / But ain't I lucky I can still breathe in."

• Close analysis: The anaphoric "A little less... A little less... A lot less... much less" enacts the cumulative subtraction of aging, then the conjunction "But" pivots the catalogue into the rhetorical question "ain't I lucky" — the speaker rewriting subtraction as luck.

• Effects: The reader experiences relief at the tonal turn — the imperatives have done their work and the speaker is no longer defending against the witness but speaking with her; the rhetorical question recruits the reader into the gratitude rather than performing it for them.

• Author's purpose: Angelou may be arguing that dignity in aging is not the refusal of limit but the right to name limit on the speaker's own terms; the closing's gratitude is earned by the opening's defiance.

• +C: The generative tension lies in the conversion: the speaker who refused pity at the opening claims luck at the close, and the two positions are not contradictions but stages of the same dignity. The poem argues that gratitude is what authority finally permits the older speaker to feel.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Angelou deploys cascading imperatives, vernacular conditional clauses, and a tonal pivot from defiance to gratitude to present aging as a process the speaker authors rather than a process performed on her."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the conversion of refusal into thankfulness — dignity in aging is not the absence of decline but the speaker's right to name the decline in her own grammar.

In both 'On Aging' and 'Jessie Emily Schofield', the speakers consider getting older. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present aging? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Jessie Emily Schofield (Judy Williams)

I used to wash my grandmother's hair,

When she was old and small

And walked with a frame

Like a learning child.

She would turn off her hearing aid

And bend into the water,

Holding the edge of the sink with long fingers;

I would pour warm cupfuls over her skull

And wonder what it could be like

In her deaf head with eighty years of life.

Hers was the softest hair I ever felt,

Wedding dress silk on a widow;

But there is a photo of her

Sitting swathed in hair

That I imagine chestnut from the black and white,

Long enough to sit on.

Her wet head felt delicate as a birdskull

Worn thin by waves of age,

As she stood bent.

My mother's mother under my hands.

• Topic sentence: Angelou presents aging through first-person imperative voice spoken by the older subject herself, the grammar of command claiming authority that the witness has attempted to withhold.

• Technique: Imperative voice cascaded across stacked exclamations, staging the older speaker as agent rather than object.

• Evidence: "Hold! Stop! Don't pity me! / Hold! Stop your sympathy!"

• Close analysis: The monosyllabic "Hold! Stop!" refuses ornament; the imperative "Don't pity me!" claims the grammatical position of command, refusing the passive position the witness had assigned.

• Effects: The reader is repositioned as the chattering witness whose interpretation must now be retracted; respect produced through grammatical reversal.

• Author's purpose: Angelou may be using imperative voice to argue that dignity in aging requires the older speaker to claim the syntax that pity would otherwise occupy on her behalf.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Angelou works through the older subject's own imperative voice, Williams works through the witness's first-person observational voice tending the older subject — aging registered as something the speaker speaks TOWARDS rather than FROM.

• Technique: First-person past-tense observation loaded with tactile imagery, the speaker positioned as caregiver narrating the older body's frailty.

• Evidence: "Her wet head felt delicate as a birdskull / Worn thin by waves of age."

• Close analysis: The simile "delicate as a birdskull" converts the older body into something held rather than commanded; "worn thin by waves of age" registers temporal erosion through natural-force metaphor, gentling the frailty without sentimentalising it.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into care rather than instructed in respect — tenderness produced through tactile imagery.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Williams may be using witness-voice tactile imagery to argue that aging is borne in part by those who tend it; the differential reveals that Angelou stages aging as a position the speaker authors from inside, while Williams stages aging as a position the speaker honours from outside.

Q3 — June 2019 — How to live your life — *The Richest Poor Man in the Valley* (Lindsay Macrae) + *Nobody* (Michael Laskey)

In 'The Richest Poor Man in the Valley', how does the poet present the speaker's attitude to how a life should be lived? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — The Richest Poor Man in the Valley (Lindsay Macrae)

On the outside

he seemed older than he was.

His face was like a weather map

full of bad weather

while inside

his heart was fat with sun.

With his two dogs

he cleared a thin silver path

across the Black Mountain.

And when winter

kicked in

they brought his sheep

down from the top

like sulky clouds.

Harry didn't care for things

that other people prize

like money, houses, bank accounts

and lies.

He was living in a caravan

until the day he died.

But at his funeral

his friends' tears

fell like a thousand

diamonds.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a celebration of a life lived simply, away from material wealth. The generative tension is the closing image: the funeral tears become "a thousand diamonds" — the conventional materialism the poem rejected is reintroduced as the metric of Harry's wealth, but transmuted from possession into mourning. The poem argues that the only true wealth is what others lose when you die. The form (irregular free-verse stanzas of accumulating length) enacts the slow accumulation of a life's worth made visible only at its end.

• Hook: "Macrae's closing converts the lexis of wealth — 'like a thousand / diamonds' — from the materialism the poem has spent four stanzas rejecting into the only metric by which a life can finally be weighed."

• Building sentence: "The poem is a free-verse elegy structured as accumulating irregular stanzas, fusing pathetic-fallacy portraiture, an asyndetic catalogue of rejected materialism, and a closing simile that transmutes the diamond-image from possession into mourning."

• Thesis: "Across irregular accumulating free-verse stanzas, a voltic move from biography into the closing simile, and weather-and-jewellery imagery that converts the rejected world's vocabulary into the only adequate audit of love, Macrae presents the speaker's attitude to how a life should be lived as a defence of meaning measured by absence rather than by accumulation."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form — irregular free-verse stanzas of accumulating length — enacts the slow accumulation of a life whose worth is visible only when the whole is in view.

• Technique: Free verse organised into five stanzas of unpredictable line-count (6, 7, 4, 2, 4), the absence of inherited regularity placing the formal weight on the architecture rather than the prosody.

• Evidence: "He was living in a caravan / until the day he died."

• Close analysis: The stanza-isolated couplet sits between the dismissive catalogue ("money, houses, bank accounts / and lies") and the closing diamonds — two lines bracketed by white space, the form making Harry's poverty unignorable; the couplet's brevity refuses to soften the fact with explanation.

• Effects: The reader registers the form's reluctance to summarise — Harry's life arrives in stanza-fragments rather than connected argument, and the stanza-isolated couplet produces a moment of arrested attention the surrounding stanzas cannot bury.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be deploying irregular free verse to argue that lives lived outside conventional metrics resist the conventional formal architectures — the form's refusal of regularity matches Harry's refusal of accumulation.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the variable stanza-lengths register the speaker's emotional rhythm rather than Harry's life — the form is mourner-shaped, not subject-shaped, and the stanza-isolated couplet is the speaker's reluctance to move past Harry's poverty rather than Harry's reluctance to leave the caravan.

• Topic: The poem's structural pivot occurs at the closing simile, where the materialist vocabulary the previous four stanzas had rejected returns transmuted from possession into mourning — and the structural inversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure landing on a closing simile, with the conjunction "But" opening the final stanza and signalling the reframe the poem has been building toward.

• Evidence: "But at his funeral / his friends' tears / fell like a thousand / diamonds."

• Close analysis: The conjunction "But" refuses the closure the previous stanzas seemed to offer (Harry's death in a caravan); the enjambed line-break between "thousand" and "diamonds" visually withholds the metric until the final word, making the reader feel the weight of the figure before its name arrives.

• Effects: The reader experiences structural relief — the rejected lexis returns as the only adequate accounting; the inversion produces a particular elation rather than sentimental grief, because the structural work has earned the simile.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be deploying the voltic closing to argue that the language of value the reader brings to the poem cannot be discarded — only redirected to count the right things; structural inversion is how the redirection is performed.

• +C: The generative tension is the relationship between rejection and recycling: the poem refuses materialism through four stanzas yet structures its argument's climax around the most materialist of all images — the rhetorical move admits that meaning cannot be made without the vocabulary the speaker disowns. Materialism is the poem's tool and its target simultaneously.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is the pairing of pathetic-fallacy weather imagery in the opening with an asyndetic list of rejected materialism in the middle — two contrasting imagery clusters that prepare the diamonds-simile to land.

• Technique: Pathetic-fallacy simile (face-as-weather-map) in the opening, asyndetic list of rejected nouns in the middle, the two clusters working as opposed semantic fields.

• Evidence: "His face was like a weather map / full of bad weather / while inside / his heart was fat with sun." paired with "like money, houses, bank accounts / and lies."

• Close analysis: "Fat with sun" yokes an adjective of material abundance to the most immaterial source of wealth available, fusing the poem's two registers in a single image; the asyndetic list ends with "lies" — three concrete material nouns and one abstraction smuggled in at the close, the syntax converting the catalogue into critique inside a single sentence.

• Effects: The reader experiences a moral recalibration produced at the level of word-choice — the opening's interior brightness and the middle's catalogue of falsehood prime the reader to accept the closing's transmuted diamonds.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be loading the imagery to argue that the world's lexis can be ethically repurposed — weather-vocabulary registers shelter, jewellery-vocabulary registers mourning, list-vocabulary registers critique.

• +C: A defensible counter-reading is that "bad weather" names real hardship the poem refuses to soften — Harry's brightness lives alongside the weather, not in defeat of it. The poem holds two positions in its language: Harry's wealth was his life's argument; Harry's poverty was its cost. The imagery does not abolish hardship, it accompanies it.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Macrae deploys pathetic-fallacy weather imagery, an asyndetic list of materialism rejected, and a final simile that converts diamonds from possession into mourning, presenting how a life should be lived as a defence of meaning measured by absence rather than by accumulation."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the recognition that the world's metrics for wealth cannot be discarded — only redirected to count the right things.

In both 'The Richest Poor Man in the Valley' and 'Nobody', the speakers present ideas about how to live a life. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these ideas? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Nobody (Michael Laskey)

If you can't bring yourself to build

a snowman or even to clench

a snowball or two to fling

at the pine tree trunk, at least

find some reason to take you

out of yourself: scrape a patch

of grass clear for the birds maybe;

prod at your shrubs so they shake off

the weight, straighten up; or just

stump about leaving prints

of your boots, your breath steaming out.

Promise.

Don't let yourself in for this moment

again: the end of the afternoon,

drawing the curtains on the glare

of the garden, a whole day of snow

nobody's trodden.

• Topic sentence: Macrae presents the argument through third-person past-tense biography, the reader positioned outside Harry's completed life and invited to weigh it once the accounting is final.

• Technique: Third-person past-tense narration loaded with the catalogue of rejected materialism, the grammar holding the reader at observational distance.

• Evidence: "Harry didn't care for things / that other people prize / like money, houses, bank accounts / and lies."

• Close analysis: The past-tense "didn't care" converts Harry into completed argument; the asyndetic list ending on "lies" converts the catalogue into critique without grammatical signposting.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into admiration — biography produces respect at distance.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be using completed-biography grammar to argue that the question of how to live is best answered by what an absence cost.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Macrae writes in completed third-person past-tense biography, Laskey writes in second-person imperative present, addressing the reader directly with instructions to be acted on today.

• Technique: Second-person imperative present, accumulating across the opening stanza, fused with the warning that closes the poem.

• Evidence: "at least / find some reason to take you / out of yourself: scrape a patch / of grass clear for the birds maybe."

• Close analysis: The imperative "find some reason" converts the reader into the agent the poem is addressing; "at least" registers the speaker's awareness that the bar is low and the failure is common.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into action — instruction produces tonight's behavioural change rather than admiration at distance.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Laskey may be using imperative-instruction grammar to argue that the question of how to live is best answered by tonight's regret avoided; the differential reveals that Macrae stages the question as life-long accounting, while Laskey stages it as a daily accounting acted on immediately.

Q4 — November 2020 — Feelings about love — *I Am Offering this Poem* (Jimmy Santiago Baca) + *The sun has burst the sky* (Jenny Joseph)

In 'I Am Offering this Poem', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about love? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — I Am Offering this Poem (Jimmy Santiago Baca)

I am offering this poem to you,

since I have nothing else to give.

Keep it like a warm coat

when winter comes to cover you,

or like a pair of thick socks

the cold cannot bite through,

I love you,

I have nothing else to give you,

so it is a pot full of yellow corn

to warm your belly in winter,

it is a scarf for your head, to wear

over your hair, to tie up around your face,

I love you,

Keep it, treasure this as you would

if you were lost, needing direction,

in the wilderness life becomes when mature;

and in the corner of your drawer,

tucked away like a cabin or hogan

in dense trees, come knocking,

and I will answer, give you directions,

and let you warm yourself by this fire,

rest by this fire, and make you feel safe

I love you,

It's all I have to give,

and all anyone needs to live,

and to go on living inside,

when the world outside

no longer cares if you live or die;

remember,

I love you.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a love poem offered as a gift in the absence of possessions. The generative tension is the closing's pivot from object-imagery to ethical imperative — "all anyone needs to live" — the poem converting from private offering into public claim. The form (accumulating extended-metaphor stanzas with the refrain "I love you" isolated between them) enacts the offering's accumulation; the speaker has nothing material to give, but the poem itself becomes the accumulated material objects through metaphor.

• Hook: "Baca opens with an admission — 'I have nothing else to give' — that the closing converts into the precise grounds on which love becomes 'all anyone needs to live'."

• Building sentence: "The poem is an extended lyric of offering, fusing accumulating practical-object metaphors, an isolated refrain that punctuates each stanza, and a closing pivot from personal gift into universal civic claim."

• Thesis: "Across an accumulating sequence of practical-object metaphors that name the body's basic needs in cold, an extended cabin-in-trees metaphor that converts the offering from clothing into shelter, and a closing pivot from personal pronoun to universal 'anyone', Baca presents the speaker's feelings about love as a substitute for material wealth that becomes the only adequate currency for survival."

• Topic: The opening stanza establishes the poem's central conceit — that the poem itself can substitute for the practical materials the speaker cannot otherwise provide.

• Technique: Sequence of practical-object similes loaded against the apologetic frame.

• Evidence: "Keep it like a warm coat / when winter comes to cover you, / or like a pair of thick socks / the cold cannot bite through."

• Close analysis: Both objects are functional rather than decorative — "warm coat", "thick socks" — naming the body's most basic needs in cold; the modifier "the cold cannot bite through" personifies cold as predator, raising the stakes of the offering.

• Effects: The reader registers the offering as urgent rather than romantic — this is love articulated as survival aid, not as decoration; the effect is tenderness mixed with anxiety, because the speaker has named the cold the offering must hold off.

• Author's purpose: Baca may be deploying the practical-object similes to mark the speaker's material poverty without naming it directly — the poem becomes the things the speaker cannot afford to buy.

• +C: The counter-reading is that the practical-object frame is itself the love — the speaker is offering not a substitute for material but the recognition that material things WERE always love, made visible by their absence.

• Topic: The middle stanzas convert the offered object from clothing into shelter, and the conversion stages love as direction-giving when the world fails.

• Technique: Extended cabin-in-trees metaphor paired with the imperative "come knocking" that activates the metaphor into an ethic of mutual obligation.

• Evidence: "in the corner of your drawer, / tucked away like a cabin or hogan / in dense trees, come knocking, / and I will answer."

• Close analysis: "Cabin or hogan" offers two cultural framings simultaneously — Anglo cabin and Navajo hogan — both naming shelter in wilderness; the imperative "come knocking" converts the poem from passive keepsake into active relationship, the speaker offering not a thing but availability.

• Effects: The reader experiences the shift from clothing-imagery to shelter-imagery as a deepening of the offering — the poem now provides not just warmth but a place; the cultural duality (cabin or hogan) refuses to specify, allowing the offering to reach across community borders.

• Author's purpose: Baca appears to deploy the dual-cultural shelter image to argue that love refuses ethnic compartmentalisation — the poem is offering itself as shelter for whoever needs it, regardless of inheritance.

• +C: The image "tucked away like a cabin or hogan / in dense trees" admits the reading that the poem must be hidden to function — love survives only when it is kept out of sight of the "world outside" the closing names; the dense trees are protection, not just setting.

• Topic: The closing stanza pivots from personal offering into universal ethical statement, and the pivot is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Conversion of the personal pronoun "you" into the universal "anyone", paired with the negative protasis "when the world outside / no longer cares if you live or die".

• Evidence: "It's all I have to give, / and all anyone needs to live, / and to go on living inside, / when the world outside / no longer cares if you live or die."

• Close analysis: The shift from "I have to give" to "anyone needs to live" converts the speaker from particular lover to universal advocate; the conditional "when the world outside / no longer cares" names the precise civic condition under which the poem must function — abandonment by the larger order.

• Effects: The reader registers the poem's stake escalating — what began as a love-gift between two has become an argument about what sustains any person against indifference; the rhetorical move converts the reader from witness to recipient.

• Author's purpose: Baca may be arguing that love is not a private accessory but a civic minimum — the only thing that allows continued living when public institutions fail.

• +C: The generative tension is that the poem began as a confession of having "nothing else to give" — material poverty — yet ends by claiming the offering is "all anyone needs". The poverty has been converted into sufficiency; the lack the poem began with is, by the close, the precondition of the abundance it offers.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Baca deploys accumulating practical-object metaphors, an isolated refrain that punctuates each stanza, and a final universal pivot to present the speaker's feelings about love as the only adequate currency for survival when public institutions fail."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the conversion of material poverty into ethical wealth — what the speaker cannot afford to give materially becomes precisely what proves love's sufficiency.

In both 'I Am Offering this Poem' and 'The sun has burst the sky', the speakers present their feelings about love. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these feelings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — The sun has burst the sky (Jenny Joseph)

The sun has burst the sky

Because I love you

And the river its banks.

The sea laps the great rocks

Because I love you

And takes no heed of the moon dragging it away

And saying coldly 'Constancy is not for you'.

The blackbird fills the air

Because I love you

With spring and lawns and shadows falling on lawns.

The people walk in the street and laugh

I love you

And far down the river ships sound their hooters

Crazy with joy because I love you.

• Topic sentence: Baca presents love through a sequence of practical-material object metaphors, converting the speaker's poverty into the catalogue of necessities the offering must replace.

• Technique: Accumulating practical-object similes (clothing, food, shelter), each grounded in the body's basic needs in cold weather.

• Evidence: "Keep it like a warm coat / when winter comes to cover you, / or like a pair of thick socks / the cold cannot bite through."

• Close analysis: "Warm coat" and "thick socks" are functional rather than decorative — naming survival rather than romance; the personification "the cold cannot bite through" casts cold as predator and raises the offering's stakes from sentiment to defence.

• Effects: The reader registers the offering as urgent rather than romantic — tenderness mixed with anxiety, because the speaker has named the cold the offering must hold off.

• Author's purpose: Baca may be using practical-object imagery to argue that love supplies what is materially absent; the poem becomes the things the speaker cannot afford to buy.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Baca presents love through practical-material substitutes for what is missing, Joseph presents love through natural cosmic events, converting the speaker's emotion into the breaking of natural limits.

• Technique: Anaphoric causal grammar — "Because I love you" repeated as the cause of cosmic effects — fused with natural-event imagery.

• Evidence: "The sun has burst the sky / Because I love you / And the river its banks."

• Close analysis: The verb "burst" converts love into rupture rather than offering; "Because I love you" repeated places love as cosmic cause, the speaker the source rather than the giver.

• Effects: The reader registers love as excess beyond proportion — exhilaration rather than the anxious tenderness Baca's necessity-imagery produces.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Joseph may be using cosmic-cause imagery to argue that love is the condition the speaker exists in, not the action the speaker performs; the differential reveals that Baca's love is provision against deprivation, while Joseph's love is excess beyond proportion — Baca's love is something done, Joseph's love is something been.

Q5 — November 2021 — A city at night — *A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M.* (Amy Lowell) + *November night, Edinburgh* (Norman MacCaig)

In 'A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M.', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about the city at night? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M. (Amy Lowell)

They have watered the street,

It shines in the glare of lamps,

Cold, white lamps,

And lies

Like a slow-moving river,

Barred with silver and black.

Cabs go down it,

One,

And then another,

Between them I hear the shuffling of feet.

Tramps doze on the window-ledges,

Night-walkers pass along the sidewalks.

The city is squalid and sinister,

With the silver-barred street in the midst,

Slow-moving,

A river leading nowhere.

Opposite my window,

The moon cuts,

Clear and round,

Through the plum-coloured night.

She cannot light the city:

It is too bright.

It has white lamps,

And glitters coldly.

I stand in the window and watch the moon.

She is thin and lustreless,

But I love her.

I know the moon,

And this is an alien city.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a description of a city at night seen from a window. The generative tension is the closing volta — the speaker's recognition of the moon ("I know the moon") and rejection of the city ("this is an alien city") — the poem converting from observation into estrangement. The form (free verse with deliberate single-word and short-line breaks that mimic the cabs going past one at a time) enacts the speaker's piecewise consumption of the city she cannot belong to.

• Hook: "Lowell's closing — 'I know the moon, / And this is an alien city' — refuses the alien city not through volume but through knowledge, the speaker's preference following what she can recognise rather than what is offered."

• Building sentence: "The poem is a window-view free-verse lyric, fusing short-line free-verse architecture, a moon-versus-lamps voltic contest, and a cold-colour semantic field loaded with metallic imagery."

• Thesis: "Across free verse whose deliberate single-word and short-line breaks mimic the cabs going past one at a time, a voltic closing turn converting observation into declared estrangement, and cold-colour semantic-field imagery that names artificial light as visually aggressive rather than illuminating, Lowell presents the speaker's feelings about the city at night as an estrangement that the artificial light cannot reach."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form is free verse organised through deliberately uneven short lines and isolated single-word lines, the form's piecewise architecture enacting the speaker's piecewise consumption of the city she cannot belong to.

• Technique: Free verse with unpredictable line-length variation — line-lengths swing from four syllables to ten — and stand-alone monosyllabic lines ("One,", "And lies") that withhold completion.

• Evidence: "Cabs go down it, / One, / And then another, / Between them I hear the shuffling of feet."

• Close analysis: "One," — a single word followed by a comma standing as its own line — visually enacts the cab passing alone, the brevity refusing the comfort of grammatical continuation; the line-break before "And then another" makes the reader wait for the next cab the way the speaker does.

• Effects: The reader registers the form somatically as the rhythm of windowed watching — each line a discrete unit, the wait between them part of the experience the form transmits.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be deploying piecewise free verse to argue that the modern city is experienced as fragments rather than narrative — the form refuses to connect what the city refuses to connect.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the free-verse fragments register the speaker's aesthetic attention rather than the city's failure — the lines slow her reading down, allowing close attention; the form's discontinuity is what licences her seeing.

• Topic: The poem's structural pivot occurs at the closing volta, where observation converts into declaration — the speaker chooses the moon she knows over the city she does not — and the structural choice is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure landing on a paired flat declarative — "I know the moon, / And this is an alien city" — preceded by the conjunction "But" that signals the structural turn.

• Evidence: "She is thin and lustreless, / But I love her. / I know the moon, / And this is an alien city."

• Close analysis: The structural turn occurs at "But" — the conjunction the previous twenty-six lines have not used; the closing paired declarative refuses qualification, the volta arriving as verdict rather than discovery.

• Effects: The reader experiences the structural arrival as moral clarity — the speaker has named her preference and what she rejects; the effect is identification rather than spectacle.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be deploying the voltic structure to argue that knowledge — what is recognised across time — is the precondition of belonging; the alien city is alien precisely because nothing in it is known the way the moon is known.

• +C: The generative tension lies in the simultaneity of love and rejection — the speaker loves a "thin and lustreless" moon and rejects a city that "glitters coldly". Brightness is no recommendation; lustre is no recommendation. The voltic structure argues that the speaker's allegiance follows her knowledge, not her seeing.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is a sustained cold-colour semantic field that converts the wet street into a metallic prison and the moon-versus-lamps rivalry into an explicit verdict on artificial light.

• Technique: Repeated colour and temperature words building a single semantic field — "Cold", "white", "silver", "black", "plum-coloured", "coldly" — fused with personifications of moon and city.

• Evidence: "Cold, white lamps, / And lies / Like a slow-moving river, / Barred with silver and black." paired with "She cannot light the city: / It is too bright."

• Close analysis: "Barred with silver and black" converts the street's reflections into prison-imagery — the bars of light and shadow alternating; "too bright" exposes the city's pathology — light excessive enough that the moon becomes invisible; the colon stages the verdict.

• Effects: The reader experiences vertigo rather than nostalgia — the natural reference point that organises night has been overruled by the artificial, and the cold-colour vocabulary cements the city as something done TO the speaker rather than seen BY her.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be loading the cold-colour semantic field to register the modern city's specific harm — not noise, not crime, but the obliteration of natural reference points by which a body knows where it is.

• +C: The image "plum-coloured night" admits the reading that the speaker has not yet lost her capacity for natural seeing — the colour-word is a Romantic register the city has not erased, and the moon's "cutting" remains visible to her even when it cannot operate as light. The language argues that her capacity to see exceeds her city's capacity to drown it.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Lowell deploys a cold-colour semantic field, free-verse line-breaks mimicking piecewise consumption of the cityscape, and a voltic recognition of the moon against the alien city to present the speaker's feelings about the city at night as estrangement that artificial light cannot reach."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the demonstration that belonging follows knowledge, not brightness — what the speaker knows orients her, regardless of how dim it has become.

In both 'A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M.' and 'November night, Edinburgh', the speakers describe a city at night. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these settings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — November night, Edinburgh (Norman MacCaig)

The night tinkles like ice in glasses.

Leaves are glued to the pavement with frost.

The brown air fumes at the shop windows,

Tries the doors, and sidles past.

I gulp down winter raw.

The heady

Darkness swirls with tenements.

In a brown fuzz of cottonwool

Lamps fade up crags, die into pits.

Frost in my lungs is harsh as leaves

Scraped up on paths.

— I look up, there,

A high roof sails, at the mast-head

Fluttering a grey and ragged star.

The world's a bear shrugged in his den.

It's snug and close in the snoring night.

And outside like chrysanthemums

The fog unfolds its bitter scent.

• Topic sentence: Lowell presents the city through cold-colour visual imagery sustained as semantic field, the reader positioned behind glass to watch but not inhabit.

• Technique: Cold-colour semantic field (silver, black, white, cold) loaded across the poem's free-verse architecture, fused with sustained visual distance.

• Evidence: "Cold, white lamps, / And lies / Like a slow-moving river, / Barred with silver and black."

• Close analysis: The triad "Cold, white lamps" refuses tonal warmth at the lexical level; "Barred with silver and black" converts the street into a prison of alternating reflections, the bars naming containment without naming a body inside them.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as witness behind glass — vertigo without touch.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be using sustained visual distance to argue that the modern city's harm is the obliteration of the natural reference points by which a body knows where it is.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Lowell presents the city through cold visual imagery observed at distance, MacCaig presents the city through synaesthetic embodied sensation — the reader inhales the city through the speaker's body.

• Technique: Synaesthetic embodied imagery (taste, breath, touch) fused with animal metaphor in the closing.

• Evidence: "I gulp down winter raw." paired with "The world's a bear shrugged in his den. / It's snug and close in the snoring night."

• Close analysis: "Gulp down winter raw" converts cold into ingested substance — winter eaten rather than seen; "a bear shrugged in his den" converts urban cold into sleeping-animal warmth, the metaphor reframing the same temperature as shelter.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into snugness — accommodation produced through embodied metaphor where Lowell produces estrangement.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: MacCaig may be using embodied animal-metaphor imagery to argue that urban night can be accommodated through the speaker's metaphorical reframing; the differential reveals that Lowell registers the city as alien through what is artificial in it, while MacCaig registers it as snug through what is animal in it.

Q6 — June 2022 — Care and responsibility — *Shoulders* (Naomi Shihab Nye) + *Choices* (Tess Gallagher)

In 'Shoulders', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about caring for someone vulnerable? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — Shoulders (Naomi Shihab Nye)

A man crosses the street in rain,

stepping gently, looking two times north and south,

because his son is asleep on his shoulder.

No car must splash him.

No car drive too near to his shadow.

This man carries the world's most sensitive cargo

but he's not marked.

Nowhere does his jacket say FRAGILE,

HANDLE WITH CARE.

His ear fills up with breathing.

He hears the hum of a boy's dream

deep inside him.

We're not going to be able to live in this world

if we're not willing to do what he's doing

with one another.

The road will only be wide.

The rain will never stop falling.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is an observation of a father carrying his sleeping son across a rainy street. The generative tension is the pivot from particular father to universal ethical claim ("We're not going to be able to live in this world") — the poem converting from observation into civic argument. The form (free verse with deliberate stanza-isolation of the central imperative and the universal claim) enacts the conversion from witness to instruction.

• Hook: "Nye's closing pivot — 'We're not going to be able to live in this world / if we're not willing to do what he's doing / with one another' — converts one observation of a father carrying his sleeping son into a structural civic claim."

• Building sentence: "The poem is a free-verse civic lyric mounted on a single observation, fusing vigilant present-tense narration, an extended commercial-cargo metaphor that exposes the absence of social care-labels, and a closing pivot from particular witnessing into universal first-person plural conscription."

• Thesis: "Across vigilant present-tense observation of the father stepping into traffic, an extended commercial-cargo metaphor whose absent FRAGILE label indicts the culture that labels packages but not parents, and a closing first-person-plural pivot that names the world's permanent rain and wide road, Nye presents the speaker's feelings about caring for someone vulnerable as an argument that ordinary tenderness is the precondition of communal survival."

• Topic: The opening stanzas establish the father's care through the vigilance of his action, rendered in present-tense observation that places the reader at the kerb beside him.

• Technique: Present-tense free verse with double-negative imperatives that exclude rather than command ("No car must... No car drive too near").

• Evidence: "A man crosses the street in rain, / stepping gently, looking two times north and south, / because his son is asleep on his shoulder. / / No car must splash him. / No car drive too near to his shadow."

• Close analysis: "Two times north and south" converts a single observation into a sustained vigilance — care is the doubled looking; the negative imperatives ("No car must") register what care is asking of the world rather than what it asks of the carer.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as the father's witness, sharing his attention to the cars he is watching; the negative imperatives produce somatic empathy — the reader's body braces with his.

• Author's purpose: Nye may be deploying the present-tense observation to argue that care is not a feeling but a discipline performed in real time — the father's love is visible only in the doubled looking.

• +C: The counter-reading is that the father is solitary in his vigilance — the negative imperatives are addressed to a world that is not listening; the care is wholly his to perform, and the poem registers the loneliness of that.

• Topic: The central stanzas pivot from the father's vigilance into the social context that fails to recognise it — the world has labels for fragile commercial goods but not for fragile human ones.

• Technique: Extended commercial-cargo metaphor paired with the absent label — capital-letter hypothetical text ("FRAGILE, HANDLE WITH CARE") that the jacket does NOT bear.

• Evidence: "This man carries the world's most sensitive cargo / but he's not marked. / Nowhere does his jacket say FRAGILE, / HANDLE WITH CARE."

• Close analysis: "World's most sensitive cargo" reframes the child in the language of logistics — the rhetorical move sets up the absent label; the capitalised "FRAGILE, HANDLE WITH CARE" is type the jacket does not bear — its absence is the visual evidence the poem produces.

• Effects: The reader registers the asymmetry — what would be marked on a parcel is not marked on a parent; the effect is indignation produced by the visible absence of the type that should be there.

• Author's purpose: Nye appears to deploy the cargo metaphor to expose the moral inadequacy of commercial-care vocabulary — society protects packages but not people; the rhetorical move converts a description of one father into an indictment of a culture.

• +C: The image "His ear fills up with breathing" — the father hearing the son's dream "deep inside him" — admits the reading that the father has labels the world does not provide; care has its own language, intimate and unverbal, and the absence of the public label is the precondition of the private hearing.

• Topic: The closing stanzas convert the particular observation into a universal civic argument and a flat statement of the persistent conditions that make care necessary — and the conversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: First-person plural pivot ("We're not going to be able to live") paired with two declarative absolutes about the world's permanence.

• Evidence: "We're not going to be able to live in this world / if we're not willing to do what he's doing / with one another. / / The road will only be wide. / The rain will never stop falling."

• Close analysis: "With one another" converts the parent-child specificity into mutual obligation between any two persons; the future-tense "will only... will never" names the world's permanence — the rain and road will outlast every interaction, so the interaction must adjust.

• Effects: The reader experiences the moral conscription — the "we" implicates the reader directly; the closing absolutes produce calm rather than panic, because the conditions are already known.

• Author's purpose: Nye may be arguing that care is not optional ornament on social life but the structural condition of social life — without it, the wide road and the falling rain win.

• +C: The generative tension lies in the relationship between the universal "we" and the particular father — the universal claim is mounted on the back of one observation, and the leap from one to all is the poem's risk. The poem argues that the leap is justified because the conditions (rain, road, vulnerability) are universal even when the labels are not.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Nye deploys vigilant present-tense observation, an extended commercial-cargo metaphor exposing the absence of social care-labels, and a pivot from particular father to universal civic ethic to present caring for the vulnerable as the precondition of communal survival."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the conversion of one father's discipline into the structural ethic by which any community survives the rain that will not stop.

In both 'Shoulders' and 'Choices', the speakers consider an act of care. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these acts of care? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Choices (Tess Gallagher)

I go to the mountain side

of the house to cut saplings,

and clear a view to snow

on the mountain. But when I look up,

saw in hand, I see a nest

clutched in the uppermost branches.

I don't cut that one.

I don't cut the others either.

Suddenly, in every tree,

an unseen nest

where a mountain would be.

• Topic sentence: Nye presents care through third-person observational voice, the carer witnessed from outside and the speaker advocating for the carer's invisible discipline.

• Technique: Third-person present-tense observation fused with negative imperatives addressed to the world beyond the carer.

• Evidence: "A man crosses the street in rain, / stepping gently, looking two times north and south, / because his son is asleep on his shoulder. / / No car must splash him. / No car drive too near to his shadow."

• Close analysis: "Two times north and south" converts vigilance into measured discipline; the negative imperatives "No car must" register what care is asking of the world rather than what care asks of the carer.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into witness-position alongside the speaker, somatically bracing with the father.

• Author's purpose: Nye may be using observational distance to argue that care is a discipline performed in real time, visible only when an outside speaker translates it into public argument.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Nye presents care through third-person observation of an outside subject, Gallagher presents care through first-person discovery — the carer IS the speaker, and care is registered as the moment a plan changes.

• Technique: First-person past-tense narration of an interrupted action, fused with the perceptual revolution that spreads from the interruption.

• Evidence: "I don't cut that one. / I don't cut the others either. / Suddenly, in every tree, / an unseen nest / where a mountain would be."

• Close analysis: The repeated "I don't cut" converts the saw-stoppage into ethical decision; "Suddenly, in every tree" generalises the discovery from one nest to all trees, the perceptual revolution arriving inside the speaker's seeing.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as the carer mid-decision rather than the witness to one.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Gallagher may be using first-person interruption-grammar to argue that care reorganises private seeing the moment it is noticed once; the differential reveals that Nye's care must be argued for publicly, while Gallagher's care propagates privately the instant the nest is seen.
